concepts.name: Corrupted npx cache causes MCP server startup failure
concepts.summary: Context7 MCP server fails at startup with a silent 'failed' state in Claude Code because `npx -y @upstash/context7-mcp` hits an EPERM error when unpacking a dependency (zod) into a corrupted/locked directory under `D:/AppData_Local/npm-cache/_npx/<hash>`. Clearing that specific _npx subdirectory is the fix.
reasoning: The plugin-level 'failed' state in Claude Code's /mcp dialog hides the underlying startup error.
Running the MCP server command directly via an initialize JSON-RPC handshake surfaces the real cause — EPERM permission denied on the npm-cache npx path, coming from a partial/locked zod unpack.
Clearing just the corrupted `_npx/<hash>` subdirectory (rather than the entire npm cache) restores the server because npx will re-fetch into a fresh working dir.
The corruption is recurring — after a restart, the same EPERM returned on Context7, suggesting an ongoing fight with antivirus / OneDrive file-locks on the D: drive npm cache path.

concepts.name: Greptile MCP plugin needs GREPTILE_API_KEY as a persistent Windows env var
concepts.summary: Greptile's MCP server uses HTTP transport with a `Bearer ${GREPTILE_API_KEY}` Authorization header; the Claude Code plugin cache at `.mcp.json` references the env var, so the fix is to set GREPTILE_API_KEY persistently via `setx` (user-scope), then revert any literal-token edits to the cached config.
reasoning: Greptile's MCP config uses `${GREPTILE_API_KEY}` substitution for the Authorization Bearer header — setting the env var is the intended path, not hardcoding the token into the cached .mcp.json.
Initial attempt to patch the literal token into the cached config is fragile because cache rebuilds will overwrite it; the marketplace source also uses ${GREPTILE_API_KEY}.
`setx` writes to HKCU only for new processes, so Claude Code must be restarted before it picks up the new env var.
Linear presents a superficially similar 'needs authentication' symptom but resolves via an OAuth browser flow triggered from /mcp — do not conflate the two.
